Generated: 2026-04-07T18:52:21.854Z

#1
ID: rw_0001
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Inference
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, inference, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Amina tested a stage scene from 1998 and a revised version prepared last spring. After the labels were shortened, readers spent 19% more time on the final section even though the topic stayed the same. Because only the wording changed, amina argues that phrasing affected attention. Still, one variable could not be isolated, so the team wants one more trial before making a permanent change.

Prompt:
Based on the passage, what can reasonably be inferred about Amina's conclusion?

Choices:
A) It proves the subject matter had no effect on reader attention in any setting.
B) It is provisional because the team wants another trial before adopting the change widely.
C) It shows the revised version will succeed equally well with every audience.
D) It confirms the original labels were inaccurate rather than merely less effective.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
C: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
D: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#2
ID: rw_0002
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Inference
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, inference, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Priya measured a translation note from 2000 and a revised version prepared last spring. After the labels were shortened, readers spent 25% more time on the final section even though the topic stayed the same. Because only the wording changed, priya argues that phrasing affected attention. Still, weather may have affected the result, so the team wants one more trial before making a permanent change.

Prompt:
Based on the passage, what can reasonably be inferred about Priya's conclusion?

Choices:
A) It proves the subject matter had no effect on reader attention in any setting.
B) It shows the revised version will succeed equally well with every audience.
C) It confirms the original labels were inaccurate rather than merely less effective.
D) It is provisional because the team wants another trial before adopting the change widely.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
B: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
C: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

Answer: A

Isaac organized a stage scene from 2019 and a revised version prepared last spring. After the labels were shortened, readers spent 57% more time on the final section even though the topic stayed the same. Because only the wording changed, isaac argues that phrasing affected attention. Still, weather may have affected the result, so the team wants one more trial before making a permanent change.

Prompt:
Based on the passage, what can reasonably be inferred about Isaac's conclusion?

Choices:
A) It proves the subject matter had no effect on reader attention in any setting.
B) It shows the revised version will succeed equally well with every audience.
C) It is provisional because the team wants another trial before adopting the change widely.
D) It confirms the original labels were inaccurate rather than merely less effective.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
B: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
D: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A) It is provisional because the team wants another trial before adopting the change widely.
B) It proves the subject matter had no effect on reader attention in any setting.
C) It shows the revised version will succeed equally well with every audience.
D) It confirms the original labels were inaccurate rather than merely less effective.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
B: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
C: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
D: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#46
ID: rw_0046

Because only the wording changed, theo argues that phrasing affected attention. Still, one variable could not be isolated, so the team wants one more trial before making a permanent change.

The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
B: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
D: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

#68
ID: rw_0068
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Inference
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, inference, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Luca revised a translation note from 2017 and a revised version prepared last spring. After the labels were shortened, readers spent 45% more time on the final section even though the topic stayed the same. Because only the wording changed, luca argues that phrasing affected attention. Still, weather may have affected the result, so the team wants one more trial before making a permanent change.

Prompt:
Based on the passage, what can reasonably be inferred about Luca's conclusion?

Choices:
A) It is provisional because the team wants another trial before adopting the change widely.
B) It proves the subject matter had no effect on reader attention in any setting.
C) It shows the revised version will succeed equally well with every audience.
D) It confirms the original labels were inaccurate rather than merely less effective.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
B: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
C: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
D: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Inference
Difficulty: hard
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 90
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, inference, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Priya measured a translation note from 2015 and a revised version prepared last spring. After the labels were shortened, readers spent 36% more time on the final section even though the topic stayed the same. Because only the wording changed, priya argues that phrasing affected attention. Still, the sample was small, so the team wants one more trial before making a permanent change.

Prompt:
Based on the passage, what can reasonably be inferred about Priya's conclusion?

Choices:
A) It proves the subject matter had no effect on reader attention in any setting.
B) It is provisional because the team wants another trial before adopting the change widely.
C) It shows the revised version will succeed equally well with every audience.
D) It confirms the original labels were inaccurate rather than merely less effective.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The passage says only the wording changed and that the team wants another trial, so the conclusion is cautious rather than final.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This choice overgeneralizes the finding beyond the single trial described.
C: The passage does not claim the result will hold for every audience.
D: The passage compares effectiveness, not factual accuracy.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#105
ID: rw_0105
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Central Idea
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, central_idea, information_and_ideas

Passage:
At first, local planners treated the narrow lot behind the community garden as unusable. Then students mapped how sunlight moved across it through the day, and residents proposed low-cost seating instead of a full playground. The final design used recycled lumber, shade cloth, and native plants. The project mattered less because it was expensive than because it showed how close observation can turn a neglected space into a useful one.

Prompt:
Which choice best states the central idea of the passage about the lot behind the community garden?

Choices:
A) Public projects succeed only when they include playground equipment.
B) Careful observation and modest design choices can transform an overlooked area into a valuable community space.
C) Students, rather than residents, should make final design decisions for neighborhood projects.
D) Recycled materials are always cheaper and more durable than new ones.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
C: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
C: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#112
ID: rw_0112
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Central Idea
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, central_idea, information_and_ideas

Passage:
At first, local planners treated the narrow lot behind the farm stand as unusable. Then students mapped how sunlight moved across it through the day, and residents proposed low-cost seating instead of a full playground. The final design used recycled lumber, shade cloth, and native plants. The project mattered less because it was expensive than because it showed how close observation can turn a neglected space into a useful one.

Prompt:
Which choice best states the central idea of the passage about the lot behind the farm stand?

Choices:
A) Public projects succeed only when they include playground equipment.
B) Students, rather than residents, should make final design decisions for neighborhood projects.
C) Recycled materials are always cheaper and more durable than new ones.
D) Careful observation and modest design choices can transform an overlooked area into a valuable community space.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
C: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
C: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#133
ID: rw_0133
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Central Idea
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, central_idea, information_and_ideas

Passage:
At first, local planners treated the narrow lot behind the robotics club as unusable. Then students mapped how sunlight moved across it through the day, and residents proposed low-cost seating instead of a full playground. The final design used recycled lumber, shade cloth, and native plants. The project mattered less because it was expensive than because it showed how close observation can turn a neglected space into a useful one.

Prompt:
Which choice best states the central idea of the passage about the lot behind the robotics club?

Choices:
A) Public projects succeed only when they include playground equipment.
B) Students, rather than residents, should make final design decisions for neighborhood projects.
C) Careful observation and modest design choices can transform an overlooked area into a valuable community space.
D) Recycled materials are always cheaper and more durable than new ones.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
C: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#154
ID: rw_0154
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Central Idea
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, central_idea, information_and_ideas

Passage:
At first, local planners treated the narrow lot behind the book drive as unusable. Then students mapped how sunlight moved across it through the day, and residents proposed low-cost seating instead of a full playground. The final design used recycled lumber, shade cloth, and native plants. The project mattered less because it was expensive than because it showed how close observation can turn a neglected space into a useful one.

Prompt:
Which choice best states the central idea of the passage about the lot behind the book drive?

Choices:
A) Public projects succeed only when they include playground equipment.
B) Careful observation and modest design choices can transform an overlooked area into a valuable community space.
C) Students, rather than residents, should make final design decisions for neighborhood projects.
D) Recycled materials are always cheaper and more durable than new ones.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
C: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#175
ID: rw_0175
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Central Idea
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, central_idea, information_and_ideas

Passage:
At first, local planners treated the narrow lot behind the city library as unusable. Then students mapped how sunlight moved across it through the day, and residents proposed low-cost seating instead of a full playground. The final design used recycled lumber, shade cloth, and native plants. The project mattered less because it was expensive than because it showed how close observation can turn a neglected space into a useful one.

Prompt:
Which choice best states the central idea of the passage about the lot behind the city library?

Choices:
A) Public projects succeed only when they include playground equipment.
B) Careful observation and modest design choices can transform an overlooked area into a valuable community space.
C) Students, rather than residents, should make final design decisions for neighborhood projects.
D) Recycled materials are always cheaper and more durable than new ones.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
C: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#196
ID: rw_0196
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Central Idea
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, central_idea, information_and_ideas

Passage:
At first, local planners treated the narrow lot behind the school store as unusable. Then students mapped how sunlight moved across it through the day, and residents proposed low-cost seating instead of a full playground. The final design used recycled lumber, shade cloth, and native plants. The project mattered less because it was expensive than because it showed how close observation can turn a neglected space into a useful one.

Prompt:
Which choice best states the central idea of the passage about the lot behind the school store?

Choices:
A) Public projects succeed only when they include playground equipment.
B) Students, rather than residents, should make final design decisions for neighborhood projects.
C) Careful observation and modest design choices can transform an overlooked area into a valuable community space.
D) Recycled materials are always cheaper and more durable than new ones.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The passage emphasizes that the lot became useful through observation and practical choices, which is the passage’s main point.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Playground equipment is never presented as necessary to the project’s success.
B: The passage describes collaboration, not a rule that students should decide alone.
D: The materials are details, not the passage’s overall focus.

#209
ID: rw_0209
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Command of Evidence
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, command_of_evidence, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Leila claims that extending the hours of the transit office would increase weekday attendance by teenagers. To evaluate that claim, the city wants evidence that isolates schedule changes from other factors, such as new programming or seasonal demand.

Prompt:
Which finding would provide the strongest evidence for Leila's claim about the transit office?

Choices:
A) Teenagers said in a survey that they generally like spaces that stay open later.
B) Attendance rose after weekday hours were extended at several similar sites, even though programming and staffing stayed the same.
C) Weekend attendance increased after one site added live music and food trucks.
D) A nearby site with unchanged hours reported that many visitors prefer afternoons to mornings.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The correct choice isolates the effect of extended hours by keeping other important variables constant.

Distractor Rationales:
A: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
C: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
D: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The correct choice isolates the effect of extended hours by keeping other important variables constant.

Distractor Rationales:
A: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
B: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
C: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

#218
ID: rw_0218
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Command of Evidence
Difficulty: hard
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 90
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, command_of_evidence, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Luca claims that extending the hours of the school store would increase weekday attendance by teenagers. To evaluate that claim, the city wants evidence that isolates schedule changes from other factors, such as new programming or seasonal demand.

Prompt:
Which finding would provide the strongest evidence for Luca's claim about the school store?

Choices:
A) Teenagers said in a survey that they generally like spaces that stay open later.
B) Attendance rose after weekday hours were extended at several similar sites, even though programming and staffing stayed the same.
C) Weekend attendance increased after one site added live music and food trucks.
D) A nearby site with unchanged hours reported that many visitors prefer afternoons to mornings.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The correct choice isolates the effect of extended hours by keeping other important variables constant.

Distractor Rationales:
A: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
C: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
D: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

B) Teenagers said in a survey that they generally like spaces that stay open later.
C) Weekend attendance increased after one site added live music and food trucks.
D) A nearby site with unchanged hours reported that many visitors prefer afternoons to mornings.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The correct choice isolates the effect of extended hours by keeping other important variables constant.

Distractor Rationales:
B: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
C: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
D: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Passage:
Mateo claims that extending the hours of the transit office would increase weekday attendance by teenagers. To evaluate that claim, the city wants evidence that isolates schedule changes from other factors, such as new programming or seasonal demand.

Prompt:
Which finding would provide the strongest evidence for Mateo's claim about the transit office?

Choices:
A) Attendance rose after weekday hours were extended at several similar sites, even though programming and staffing stayed the same.
B) Teenagers said in a survey that they generally like spaces that stay open later.
C) Weekend attendance increased after one site added live music and food trucks.
D) A nearby site with unchanged hours reported that many visitors prefer afternoons to mornings.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The correct choice isolates the effect of extended hours by keeping other important variables constant.

Distractor Rationales:
B: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
C: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
D: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ID: rw_0289
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Command of Evidence
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, command_of_evidence, information_and_ideas

Passage:
Leila claims that extending the hours of the transit office would increase weekday attendance by teenagers. To evaluate that claim, the city wants evidence that isolates schedule changes from other factors, such as new programming or seasonal demand.

Prompt:
Which finding would provide the strongest evidence for Leila's claim about the transit office?

Choices:
A) Teenagers said in a survey that they generally like spaces that stay open later.
B) Weekend attendance increased after one site added live music and food trucks.
C) Attendance rose after weekday hours were extended at several similar sites, even though programming and staffing stayed the same.
D) A nearby site with unchanged hours reported that many visitors prefer afternoons to mornings.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The correct choice isolates the effect of extended hours by keeping other important variables constant.

Distractor Rationales:
A: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
B: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
D: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A: A preference survey does not show that changing hours actually increased attendance.
B: Programming changes introduce a confounding variable.
C: This finding does not compare attendance before and after extended hours.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#313
ID: rw_0313
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Quantitative Information in Text and Graphics
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, quantitative_information_in_text_and_graphics, information_and_ideas

Passage:
A district report compared average daily attendance at four after-school workshops. Debate club averaged 24 students, robotics averaged 36, mural design averaged 34, and journalism averaged 46. The report argues that workshops combining collaboration with a concrete final product drew stronger attendance than workshops focused mainly on discussion.

Prompt:
Which choice best uses the data in the passage to evaluate the report’s argument?

Choices:
A) The data prove that every student prefers journalism to all other workshop options.
B) The report’s argument cannot be evaluated because the attendance numbers are all different.
C) The data generally support the argument because robotics and journalism, which produce tangible projects, both outdrew debate club.
D) The data show only that mural design should be canceled next year.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The best answer connects the attendance figures to the report’s claim instead of making a broader claim the data do not support.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The data do not prove universal student preference.
B: Different numbers do allow a comparison tied to the report’s claim.
D: The figures alone do not justify eliminating a workshop.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

C) The data generally support the argument because robotics and journalism, which produce tangible projects, both outdrew debate club.
D) The data show only that mural design should be canceled next year.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The best answer connects the attendance figures to the report’s claim instead of making a broader claim the data do not support.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The data do not prove universal student preference.
B: Different numbers do allow a comparison tied to the report’s claim.
D: The figures alone do not justify eliminating a workshop.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Passage:
A district report compared average daily attendance at four after-school workshops. Debate club averaged 33 students, robotics averaged 43, mural design averaged 39, and journalism averaged 52. The report argues that workshops combining collaboration with a concrete final product drew stronger attendance than workshops focused mainly on discussion.

Prompt:
Which choice best uses the data in the passage to evaluate the report’s argument?

Choices:
A) The data prove that every student prefers journalism to all other workshop options.
B) The report’s argument cannot be evaluated because the attendance numbers are all different.
C) The data show only that mural design should be canceled next year.
D) The data generally support the argument because robotics and journalism, which produce tangible projects, both outdrew debate club.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The best answer connects the attendance figures to the report’s claim instead of making a broader claim the data do not support.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The data do not prove universal student preference.
B: Different numbers do allow a comparison tied to the report’s claim.
C: The figures alone do not justify eliminating a workshop.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Estimated Time (s): 90
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, quantitative_information_in_text_and_graphics, information_and_ideas

Passage:
A district report compared average daily attendance at four after-school workshops. Debate club averaged 21 students, robotics averaged 35, mural design averaged 33, and journalism averaged 47. The report argues that workshops combining collaboration with a concrete final product drew stronger attendance than workshops focused mainly on discussion.

Prompt:
Which choice best uses the data in the passage to evaluate the report’s argument?

Choices:
A) The data prove that every student prefers journalism to all other workshop options.
B) The report’s argument cannot be evaluated because the attendance numbers are all different.
C) The data show only that mural design should be canceled next year.
D) The data generally support the argument because robotics and journalism, which produce tangible projects, both outdrew debate club.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The best answer connects the attendance figures to the report’s claim instead of making a broader claim the data do not support.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The data do not prove universal student preference.
B: Different numbers do allow a comparison tied to the report’s claim.
C: The figures alone do not justify eliminating a workshop.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#400
ID: rw_0400
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Information and Ideas
Skill: Quantitative Information in Text and Graphics
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, quantitative_information_in_text_and_graphics, information_and_ideas

Passage:
A district report compared average daily attendance at four after-school workshops. Debate club averaged 26 students, robotics averaged 35, mural design averaged 35, and journalism averaged 43. The report argues that workshops combining collaboration with a concrete final product drew stronger attendance than workshops focused mainly on discussion.

Prompt:
Which choice best uses the data in the passage to evaluate the report’s argument?

Choices:
A) The data generally support the argument because robotics and journalism, which produce tangible projects, both outdrew debate club.
B) The data prove that every student prefers journalism to all other workshop options.
C) The report’s argument cannot be evaluated because the attendance numbers are all different.
D) The data show only that mural design should be canceled next year.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The best answer connects the attendance figures to the report’s claim instead of making a broader claim the data do not support.

Distractor Rationales:
B: The data do not prove universal student preference.
C: Different numbers do allow a comparison tied to the report’s claim.
D: The figures alone do not justify eliminating a workshop.

Calculator Allowed:

Tags: history/social studies, words_in_context, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Jonah's commentary on the census summary was deliberately plain. Rather than praise every element, the writer noted what the project accomplished, where its evidence thinned, and how its final claim depended on a small archive. The tone stayed respectful, but it avoided broad celebration.

Prompt:
As used in the passage, what does "plain" most nearly mean?

Choices:
A) ordinary in appearance
B) direct and uncomplicated
C) located on open land
D) lacking any value

Answer: B

Rationale:
The surrounding description explains the word through the writer’s tone and method, indicating its contextual meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This uses a different meaning of the word than the one implied by the passage.
C: This meaning does not fit the writer’s analytical tone in context.
D: This choice ignores the explanatory clues surrounding the word.

Rationale:
The surrounding description explains the word through the writer’s tone and method, indicating its contextual meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This uses a different meaning of the word than the one implied by the passage.
B: This meaning does not fit the writer’s analytical tone in context.
C: This choice ignores the explanatory clues surrounding the word.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A) painfully critical
B) sudden in timing
C) precise and clearly defined
D) physically pointed

Answer: C

Rationale:
The surrounding description explains the word through the writer’s tone and method, indicating its contextual meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This uses a different meaning of the word than the one implied by the passage.
B: This meaning does not fit the writer’s analytical tone in context.
D: This choice ignores the explanatory clues surrounding the word.

Leila's commentary on the observation note was deliberately reserve. Rather than praise every element, the writer noted what the project accomplished, where its evidence thinned, and how its final claim depended on a small archive. The tone stayed respectful, but it avoided broad celebration.

Prompt:
As used in the passage, what does "reserve" most nearly mean?

Choices:
A) book in advance
B) hold back rather than reveal fully
C) save for future use
D) an area protected by law

Answer: B

Rationale:
The surrounding description explains the word through the writer’s tone and method, indicating its contextual meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This uses a different meaning of the word than the one implied by the passage.
C: This meaning does not fit the writer’s analytical tone in context.
D: This choice ignores the explanatory clues surrounding the word.

#529
ID: rw_0529
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Text Structure
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, text_structure, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Researchers once described the marsh as a seasonal stopover. The passage first summarizes that earlier view and then introduces a decade of tracking data showing that some birds remain year-round. It closes by explaining how the updated finding affects local restoration plans.

Prompt:
Which choice best describes the structure of the passage?

Choices:
A) It presents an older understanding, introduces new evidence, and then explains a practical implication of that evidence.
B) It lists several unrelated discoveries in chronological order without evaluating them.
C) It compares two marshes and argues that one should replace the other.
D) It gives a personal anecdote before shifting to a broad philosophical reflection.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
B: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
C: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
D: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#530
ID: rw_0530
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Text Structure
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, text_structure, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Researchers once described the marsh as a seasonal stopover. The passage first summarizes that earlier view and then introduces a decade of tracking data showing that some birds remain year-round. It closes by explaining how the updated finding affects local restoration plans.

Prompt:
Which choice best describes the structure of the passage?

Choices:
A) It lists several unrelated discoveries in chronological order without evaluating them.
B) It compares two marshes and argues that one should replace the other.
C) It presents an older understanding, introduces new evidence, and then explains a practical implication of that evidence.
D) It gives a personal anecdote before shifting to a broad philosophical reflection.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
B: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
D: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

D: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

#555
ID: rw_0555
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Text Structure
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, text_structure, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Researchers once described the marsh as a seasonal stopover. The passage first summarizes that earlier view and then introduces a decade of tracking data showing that some birds remain year-round. It closes by explaining how the updated finding affects local restoration plans.

Prompt:
Which choice best describes the structure of the passage?

Choices:
A) It lists several unrelated discoveries in chronological order without evaluating them.
B) It compares two marshes and argues that one should replace the other.
C) It gives a personal anecdote before shifting to a broad philosophical reflection.
D) It presents an older understanding, introduces new evidence, and then explains a practical implication of that evidence.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
B: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
C: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
C: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
D: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#580
ID: rw_0580
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Text Structure
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, text_structure, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Researchers once described the marsh as a seasonal stopover. The passage first summarizes that earlier view and then introduces a decade of tracking data showing that some birds remain year-round. It closes by explaining how the updated finding affects local restoration plans.

Prompt:
Which choice best describes the structure of the passage?

Choices:
A) It lists several unrelated discoveries in chronological order without evaluating them.
B) It compares two marshes and argues that one should replace the other.
C) It gives a personal anecdote before shifting to a broad philosophical reflection.
D) It presents an older understanding, introduces new evidence, and then explains a practical implication of that evidence.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
B: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
C: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

D) It gives a personal anecdote before shifting to a broad philosophical reflection.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
C: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
D: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A) It lists several unrelated discoveries in chronological order without evaluating them.
B) It compares two marshes and argues that one should replace the other.
C) It presents an older understanding, introduces new evidence, and then explains a practical implication of that evidence.
D) It gives a personal anecdote before shifting to a broad philosophical reflection.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The passage clearly moves from prior view to new evidence to an application of the updated understanding.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage is not simply a list of unrelated discoveries.
B: No comparison between two marshes appears in the passage.
D: The passage is analytical, not personal or philosophical.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Researchers once described the marsh as a seasonal stopover. The passage first summarizes that earlier view and then introduces a decade of tracking data showing that some birds remain year-round. It closes by explaining how the updated finding affects local restoration plans.

#641
ID: rw_0641
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Rhetorical Purpose
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, rhetorical_purpose, craft_and_structure

Passage:
In arguing that the archive was assembled in stages, the historian pauses to mention a cracked margin note. That detail does not prove the entire case by itself, but it shows that at least one section was added after the first round of copying, supporting the historian’s broader claim about revision.

Prompt:
Why does the author include the detail about a cracked margin note?

Choices:
A) To show that the archive is too damaged to study responsibly.
B) To provide a specific example that supports the claim that the archive changed over time.
C) To argue that revision always improves historical documents.
D) To shift the passage from evidence-based analysis to personal opinion.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The detail functions as a concrete supporting example tied directly to the historian’s broader argument.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage does not suggest the archive is unusable.
C: The author is not making a general claim that revision is always beneficial.
D: The tone remains analytical throughout.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, rhetorical_purpose, craft_and_structure

Passage:
In arguing that the archive was assembled in stages, the historian pauses to mention a revised map legend. That detail does not prove the entire case by itself, but it shows that at least one section was added after the first round of copying, supporting the historian’s broader claim about revision.

Prompt:
Why does the author include the detail about a revised map legend?

Choices:
A) To show that the archive is too damaged to study responsibly.
B) To argue that revision always improves historical documents.
C) To shift the passage from evidence-based analysis to personal opinion.
D) To provide a specific example that supports the claim that the archive changed over time.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The detail functions as a concrete supporting example tied directly to the historian’s broader argument.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage does not suggest the archive is unusable.
B: The author is not making a general claim that revision is always beneficial.
C: The tone remains analytical throughout.

A) To provide a specific example that supports the claim that the archive changed over time.
B) To show that the archive is too damaged to study responsibly.
C) To argue that revision always improves historical documents.
D) To shift the passage from evidence-based analysis to personal opinion.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The detail functions as a concrete supporting example tied directly to the historian’s broader argument.

Distractor Rationales:
B: The passage does not suggest the archive is unusable.
C: The author is not making a general claim that revision is always beneficial.
D: The tone remains analytical throughout.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In arguing that the archive was assembled in stages, the historian pauses to mention a single weather reading. That detail does not prove the entire case by itself, but it shows that at least one section was added after the first round of copying, supporting the historian’s broader claim about revision.

Prompt:
Why does the author include the detail about a single weather reading?

Choices:
A) To show that the archive is too damaged to study responsibly.
B) To argue that revision always improves historical documents.
C) To provide a specific example that supports the claim that the archive changed over time.
D) To shift the passage from evidence-based analysis to personal opinion.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The detail functions as a concrete supporting example tied directly to the historian’s broader argument.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage does not suggest the archive is unusable.
B: The author is not making a general claim that revision is always beneficial.
D: The tone remains analytical throughout.

C) To argue that revision always improves historical documents.
D) To shift the passage from evidence-based analysis to personal opinion.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The detail functions as a concrete supporting example tied directly to the historian’s broader argument.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage does not suggest the archive is unusable.
C: The author is not making a general claim that revision is always beneficial.
D: The tone remains analytical throughout.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#752
ID: rw_0752
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Rhetorical Purpose
Difficulty: hard
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 90
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, rhetorical_purpose, craft_and_structure

Passage:
In arguing that the archive was assembled in stages, the historian pauses to mention a donation ledger. That detail does not prove the entire case by itself, but it shows that at least one section was added after the first round of copying, supporting the historian’s broader claim about revision.

Prompt:
Why does the author include the detail about a donation ledger?

Choices:
A) To show that the archive is too damaged to study responsibly.
B) To argue that revision always improves historical documents.
C) To shift the passage from evidence-based analysis to personal opinion.
D) To provide a specific example that supports the claim that the archive changed over time.

Answer: D

Rationale:
The detail functions as a concrete supporting example tied directly to the historian’s broader argument.

Distractor Rationales:
A: The passage does not suggest the archive is unusable.
B: The author is not making a general claim that revision is always beneficial.
C: The tone remains analytical throughout.

#753
ID: rw_0753
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Cross-Text Connections
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, cross_text_connections, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Text 1: A local columnist argues that the town should repaint its century-old water tower in bright colors so it can serve as a landmark for visitors. Text 2: A preservation architect agrees that the tower should remain prominent, but argues that a careful cleaning and lighting plan would preserve its industrial character while still making it visible at night.

Prompt:
What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.
C) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
D) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.

Answer: B

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
C: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
D: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

B) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.
C) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
D) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.

Answer: B

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
C: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
D: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ID: rw_0774

Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, cross_text_connections, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Text 1: A local columnist argues that the town should repaint its century-old water tower in bright colors so it can serve as a landmark for visitors. Text 2: A preservation architect agrees that the tower should remain prominent, but argues that a careful cleaning and lighting plan would preserve its industrial character while still making it visible at night.

Prompt:
What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.
C) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.

Distractor Rationales:

C: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
D: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
C) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.
D) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.

Answer: D

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
B: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
C: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#796
ID: rw_0796
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Cross-Text Connections
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, cross_text_connections, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Text 1: A local columnist argues that the town should repaint its century-old water tower in bright colors so it can serve as a landmark for visitors. Text 2: A preservation architect agrees that the tower should remain prominent, but argues that a careful cleaning and lighting plan would preserve its industrial character while still making it visible at night.

Prompt:
What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
C) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.
D) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.

Answer: C

Rationale:

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
B: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
D: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
C) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.
D) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.

Answer: D

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
B: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
C: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#818
ID: rw_0818
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Cross-Text Connections
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, cross_text_connections, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Text 1: A local columnist argues that the town should repaint its century-old water tower in bright colors so it can serve as a landmark for visitors. Text 2: A preservation architect agrees that the tower should remain prominent, but argues that a careful cleaning and lighting plan would preserve its industrial character while still making it visible at night.

Prompt:
What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:

What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.
C) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
D) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.

Answer: B

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
C: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
D: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ID: rw_0840

Text 1: A local columnist argues that the town should repaint its century-old water tower in bright colors so it can serve as a landmark for visitors. Text 2: A preservation architect agrees that the tower should remain prominent, but argues that a careful cleaning and lighting plan would preserve its industrial character while still making it visible at night.

Prompt:

Prompt:
What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
C) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.
D) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.

Answer: D

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
B: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
C: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#862
ID: rw_0862
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Craft and Structure
Skill: Cross-Text Connections
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, cross_text_connections, craft_and_structure

Passage:
Text 1: A local columnist argues that the town should repaint its century-old water tower in bright colors so it can serve as a landmark for visitors. Text 2: A preservation architect agrees that the tower should remain prominent, but argues that a careful cleaning and lighting plan would preserve its industrial character while still making it visible at night.

Prompt:
What would the author of Text 2 most likely say in response to the proposal in Text 1?

Choices:
A) Visitors should decide the tower’s final design because residents are too invested in its history.
B) The tower should be removed because both appearance and maintenance are too costly.
C) Bright paint is the only realistic way to make the tower noticeable after dark.
D) Visibility matters, but the tower should be highlighted in a way that preserves its historical character.

Answer: D

Rationale:
Text 2 accepts the goal of visibility but proposes a different method that protects the tower’s character.

Distractor Rationales:
A: Text 2 does not dismiss residents or transfer decision-making authority.
B: Text 2 argues for preservation, not removal.
C: Text 2 explicitly offers an alternative to bright paint.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#865
ID: rw_0865
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Transitions
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, transitions, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
The volunteers simplified the sign-in form for the community garden. ____ , new participants completed registration about 28% faster during the next event.

Prompt:
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

Choices:
A) Specifically
B) Nevertheless
C) As a result
D) Also

Answer: D

Rationale:
The correct transition matches the logical relationship between the two parts of the sentence.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This transition signals a different relationship than the one expressed in the sentence.
B: This choice would mislead the reader about how the second clause connects to the first.
C: The sentence does not support the relationship implied by this transition.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tags: history/social studies, transitions, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
The volunteers simplified the sign-in form for the transit office. ____ , the organizers also added a clearly marked supply table near the entrance.

Prompt:
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

Choices:
A) For instance
B) For example
C) Also
D) Accordingly

Answer: B

Rationale:
The correct transition matches the logical relationship between the two parts of the sentence.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This transition signals a different relationship than the one expressed in the sentence.
C: This choice would mislead the reader about how the second clause connects to the first.
D: The sentence does not support the relationship implied by this transition.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Rationale:
The correct transition matches the logical relationship between the two parts of the sentence.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This transition signals a different relationship than the one expressed in the sentence.
B: This choice would mislead the reader about how the second clause connects to the first.
D: The sentence does not support the relationship implied by this transition.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#945
ID: rw_0945
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Rhetorical Synthesis
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, rhetorical_synthesis, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
A student wants to explain that the lighting change at the robotics club saved energy and paid for itself quickly. The student’s notes are below.
- robotics club replaced fluorescent bulbs with LEDs in 2020.
- Electricity use in the building fell by 22% during the next full year.
- The manager said the quieter fixtures made evening programs feel calmer.
- Installation costs were recovered in about 2 years.

Prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses the notes to accomplish the student’s goal?

Choices:
A) robotics club changed its lighting in 2020, and the manager later described the new fixtures as quieter.
B) After robotics club switched to LED lighting, its electricity use fell by 22%, and the installation cost was recovered in about 2 years.
C) The manager of robotics club preferred the calmer mood created by the new lights, which explains why all building renovations should begin with aesthetics.
D) robotics club installed LEDs, a type of light used in many buildings, and electricity use changed during the following year.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The correct choice directly addresses both parts of the goal: energy savings and rapid cost recovery.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option omits the information about energy savings and payback time.
C: This option adds an unsupported generalization that goes beyond the notes.
D: This option is too vague to accomplish the stated goal precisely.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

- Installation costs were recovered in about 3 years.

Prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses the notes to accomplish the student’s goal?

Choices:
A) book drive changed its lighting in 2019, and the manager later described the new fixtures as quieter.
B) After book drive switched to LED lighting, its electricity use fell by 15%, and the installation cost was recovered in about 3 years.
C) The manager of book drive preferred the calmer mood created by the new lights, which explains why all building renovations should begin with aesthetics.
D) book drive installed LEDs, a type of light used in many buildings, and electricity use changed during the following year.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The correct choice directly addresses both parts of the goal: energy savings and rapid cost recovery.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option omits the information about energy savings and payback time.
C: This option adds an unsupported generalization that goes beyond the notes.
D: This option is too vague to accomplish the stated goal precisely.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

D) robotics club installed LEDs, a type of light used in many buildings, and electricity use changed during the following year.

Answer: A

Rationale:
The correct choice directly addresses both parts of the goal: energy savings and rapid cost recovery.

Distractor Rationales:
B: This option omits the information about energy savings and payback time.
C: This option adds an unsupported generalization that goes beyond the notes.
D: This option is too vague to accomplish the stated goal precisely.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#970
ID: rw_0970
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Rhetorical Synthesis
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, rhetorical_synthesis, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
A student wants to explain that the lighting change at the science fair saved energy and paid for itself quickly. The student’s notes are below.
- science fair replaced fluorescent bulbs with LEDs in 2021.
- Electricity use in the building fell by 23% during the next full year.
- The manager said the quieter fixtures made evening programs feel calmer.
- Installation costs were recovered in about 3 years.

Prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses the notes to accomplish the student’s goal?

Choices:
A) science fair changed its lighting in 2021, and the manager later described the new fixtures as quieter.
B) The manager of science fair preferred the calmer mood created by the new lights, which explains why all building renovations should begin with aesthetics.
C) After science fair switched to LED lighting, its electricity use fell by 23%, and the installation cost was recovered in about 3 years.
D) science fair installed LEDs, a type of light used in many buildings, and electricity use changed during the following year.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The correct choice directly addresses both parts of the goal: energy savings and rapid cost recovery.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option omits the information about energy savings and payback time.
B: This option adds an unsupported generalization that goes beyond the notes.
D: This option is too vague to accomplish the stated goal precisely.

#989
ID: rw_0989
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Rhetorical Synthesis
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, rhetorical_synthesis, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
A student wants to explain that the lighting change at the community garden saved energy and paid for itself quickly. The student’s notes are below.
- community garden replaced fluorescent bulbs with LEDs in 2022.
- Electricity use in the building fell by 18% during the next full year.
- The manager said the quieter fixtures made evening programs feel calmer.
- Installation costs were recovered in about 2 years.

Prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses the notes to accomplish the student’s goal?

Choices:
A) community garden changed its lighting in 2022, and the manager later described the new fixtures as quieter.
B) After community garden switched to LED lighting, its electricity use fell by 18%, and the installation cost was recovered in about 2 years.
C) The manager of community garden preferred the calmer mood created by the new lights, which explains why all building renovations should begin with aesthetics.
D) community garden installed LEDs, a type of light used in many buildings, and electricity use changed during the following year.

Answer: B

Rationale:
The correct choice directly addresses both parts of the goal: energy savings and rapid cost recovery.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option omits the information about energy savings and payback time.
C: This option adds an unsupported generalization that goes beyond the notes.
D: This option is too vague to accomplish the stated goal precisely.

Source Context: humanities
Tags: humanities, rhetorical_synthesis, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
A student wants to explain that the lighting change at the school store saved energy and paid for itself quickly. The student’s notes are below.
- school store replaced fluorescent bulbs with LEDs in 2023.
- Electricity use in the building fell by 25% during the next full year.
- The manager said the quieter fixtures made evening programs feel calmer.
- Installation costs were recovered in about 5 years.

Prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses the notes to accomplish the student’s goal?

Choices:
A) school store changed its lighting in 2023, and the manager later described the new fixtures as quieter.
B) The manager of school store preferred the calmer mood created by the new lights, which explains why all building renovations should begin with aesthetics.
C) After school store switched to LED lighting, its electricity use fell by 25%, and the installation cost was recovered in about 5 years.
D) school store installed LEDs, a type of light used in many buildings, and electricity use changed during the following year.

Answer: C

Rationale:
The correct choice directly addresses both parts of the goal: energy savings and rapid cost recovery.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option omits the information about energy savings and payback time.
B: This option adds an unsupported generalization that goes beyond the notes.
D: This option is too vague to accomplish the stated goal precisely.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#1025
ID: rw_1025
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Sentence Placement
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: history/social studies
Tags: history/social studies, sentence_placement, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
[1] The museum tested a new foldout map for its sculpture garden. [2] Designers replaced dense paragraph labels with short directional cues. [3] Volunteers then asked guests to find three installations without staff help. [4] On average, guests completed the route in less time than they had with the old map.

Prompt:
Where should the sentence "This made the final map easier for first-time visitors to use." be placed in the passage?

Choices:
A) After sentence 2
B) After sentence 1
C) After sentence 3
D) After sentence 4

Answer: A

Rationale:
The inserted sentence logically refers to the label changes in sentence 2 and sets up the usability test described next.

Distractor Rationales:
B: At that point, the sentence would not yet have a clear referent for “This.”
C: By then, the sentence interrupts the test sequence instead of clarifying the revision.
D: Placed there, the sentence repeats the result rather than connecting the revision to the test.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#1028
ID: rw_1028
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Sentence Placement
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, sentence_placement, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
[1] The museum tested a new foldout map for its sculpture garden. [2] Designers replaced dense paragraph labels with short directional cues. [3] Volunteers then asked guests to find three installations without staff help. [4] On average, guests completed the route in less time than they had with the old map.

Prompt:
Where should the sentence "This made the final map easier for first-time visitors to use." be placed in the passage?

Choices:
A) After sentence 2
B) After sentence 1
C) After sentence 3
D) After sentence 4

Answer: A

Rationale:
The inserted sentence logically refers to the label changes in sentence 2 and sets up the usability test described next.

Distractor Rationales:
B: At that point, the sentence would not yet have a clear referent for “This.”
C: By then, the sentence interrupts the test sequence instead of clarifying the revision.
D: Placed there, the sentence repeats the result rather than connecting the revision to the test.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#1052
ID: rw_1052
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Sentence Placement
Difficulty: medium
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 75
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, sentence_placement, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
[1] The museum tested a new foldout map for its sculpture garden. [2] Designers replaced dense paragraph labels with short directional cues. [3] Volunteers then asked guests to find three installations without staff help. [4] On average, guests completed the route in less time than they had with the old map.

Prompt:
Where should the sentence "This made the final map easier for first-time visitors to use." be placed in the passage?

Choices:
A) After sentence 1
B) After sentence 3
C) After sentence 4
D) After sentence 2

Answer: D

Rationale:
The inserted sentence logically refers to the label changes in sentence 2 and sets up the usability test described next.

B: By then, the sentence interrupts the test sequence instead of clarifying the revision.

Calculator Allowed:

B) After sentence 3
C) After sentence 4
D) After sentence 2

Answer: D

Rationale:
The inserted sentence logically refers to the label changes in sentence 2 and sets up the usability test described next.

Distractor Rationales:
A: At that point, the sentence would not yet have a clear referent for “This.”
B: By then, the sentence interrupts the test sequence instead of clarifying the revision.
C: Placed there, the sentence repeats the result rather than connecting the revision to the test.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#1100
ID: rw_1100

Skill: Sentence Placement
Difficulty: easy
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 65
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: literature
Tags: literature, sentence_placement, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
[1] The museum tested a new foldout map for its sculpture garden. [2] Designers replaced dense paragraph labels with short directional cues. [3] Volunteers then asked guests to find three installations without staff help. [4] On average, guests completed the route in less time than they had with the old map.

#1105
ID: rw_1105
Section: reading_writing
Domain: Expression of Ideas
Skill: Concision
Difficulty: hard
Format: mc
Estimated Time (s): 90
Calculator Allowed: 
Source Context: science
Tags: science, concision, expression_of_ideas

Passage:
The newsletter explains that the student volunteers at the city library "worked together collaboratively" to sort donated books by reading level before the event opened.

Prompt:
Which choice most effectively revises the quoted portion to make the sentence concise while preserving its meaning?

Choices:
A) collaboratively worked together with one another
B) worked in a collaborative way together
C) worked in collaboration together
D) worked together

Answer: D

Rationale:
The correct choice removes unnecessary repetition while keeping the original meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option repeats the same idea with extra words.
B: This option is wordy and keeps redundant phrasing.
C: This option still repeats the idea of collaboration unnecessarily.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Which choice most effectively revises the quoted portion to make the sentence concise while preserving its meaning?

Choices:
A) collaboratively worked together with one another
B) worked in a collaborative way together
C) worked together
D) worked in collaboration together

Answer: C

Rationale:
The correct choice removes unnecessary repetition while keeping the original meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option repeats the same idea with extra words.
B: This option is wordy and keeps redundant phrasing.
D: This option still repeats the idea of collaboration unnecessarily.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

C) worked in collaboration together
D) worked together

Answer: D

Rationale:
The correct choice removes unnecessary repetition while keeping the original meaning.

Distractor Rationales:
A: This option repeats the same idea with extra words.
B: This option is wordy and keeps redundant phrasing.
C: This option still repeats the idea of collaboration unnecessarily.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
